title: Stallings Foldings and Subgroups of Free Groups
overall_summary: The paper gives a combinatorial account of Stallings foldings for subgroups of finitely generated free groups. Its central construction represents subgroups of F(X) by based X-labeled directed graphs, folds pairs of equally labeled edges with the same initial vertex, and extracts a canonical folded core graph whose reduced loop labels are exactly the subgroup. The most relevant content for the original problem is the rigorous treatment of free cancellation as a sequence of local, complexity-decreasing foldings that preserve the represented free-group element or subgroup, together with deterministic path-reading in folded graphs. The spanning-tree and Nielsen-reduced-basis arguments also provide concrete cancellation estimates and normal forms for reduced loops, which may be useful if matchings are encoded as cancellation histories or graph quotients.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be a finite alphabet, let Γ be an X-labeled directed graph, and let ^hatΓ denote the graph obtained by adjoining to every directed edge e a formal inverse edge e^{-1} with inverse label. Suppose Γ1 is obtained from Γ by one Stallings folding: two distinct edges f1 and f2 of ^hatΓ with the same origin and the same label x in X ∪ X^{-1} are identified, and their terminal vertices are identified, producing a new X-digraph Γ1. If v is a vertex of Γ and v1 is its image in Γ1, then: (1) if Γ is connected, Γ1 is connected; (2) every path p in Γ from v to v with label w maps edgewise to a path in Γ1 from v1 to v1 with the same label w; and (3) if Γ is finite, a folding decreases the number of positive edges of Γ by one.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The folding is a quotient operation on the underlying graph: it identifies the two chosen equally labeled directed edges and identifies the corresponding terminal vertices. A quotient of a connected graph is connected, giving (1). Since the quotient map sends edges to edges with the same labels and preserves origins and termini after the identifications, the edgewise image of any loop based at v is a loop based at v1 with exactly the same sequence of labels, giving (2). In the positive edge set of the original X-digraph, the two positive representatives of the folded pair are replaced by one new positive edge, while all other positive edges remain; hence the number of positive edges drops by one, giving (3).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.9
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a folded X-digraph, meaning that for every vertex q of Γ and every letter x in X ∪ X^{-1}, there is at most one edge of ^hatΓ with origin q and label x. If p is a reduced path in Γ, meaning p has no consecutive subpath of the form e,e^{-1}, then the label of p is a freely reduced word in X ∪ X^{-1}. Consequently, for any base vertex v, every word in the language L(Γ,v), defined as the set of labels of reduced loops at v, is freely reduced.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If the label of a reduced path contained a free cancellation xx^{-1}, then for some consecutive edges e_i,e_{i+1} of the path, μ(e_i)=x and μ(e_{i+1})=x^{-1}. At the common vertex t(e_i)=o(e_{i+1}), the two edges e_i^{-1} and e_{i+1} both have origin t(e_i) and label x^{-1}. Since Γ is folded, they must be the same edge, so e_{i+1}=e_i^{-1}. This contradicts that the path is reduced. Thus no adjacent inverse labels occur.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.1 and Lemma 3.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be an X-digraph and v a base vertex. Let L(Γ,v) be the set of labels of reduced paths in Γ from v to v, where labels are words in X ∪ X^{-1}. Let overline{L(Γ,v)} be the set of freely reduced forms of all words in L(Γ,v), viewed as elements of the free group F(X). Then overline{L(Γ,v)} is a subgroup of F(X). If Γ is folded, then every word in L(Γ,v) is already freely reduced, and L(Γ,v)=overline{L(Γ,v)} is a subgroup of F(X).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For closure under multiplication, take two reduced loops p1,p2 at v with labels w1,w2. Their concatenation p1p2 is a loop at v, possibly not path-reduced. Perform all possible path reductions to obtain a reduced loop p. The label of p freely reduces to the product of the freely reduced labels of p1 and p2, so the product lies in overline{L(Γ,v)}. For inverses, reverse a reduced loop p and replace each edge by its formal inverse; the resulting reduced loop has label representing the inverse element. The empty path gives the identity. If Γ is folded, Lemma 2.9 says labels of reduced loops are freely reduced, so no passage to freely reduced forms is needed.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ′ be obtained from an X-digraph Γ by a single Stallings folding, and let v′ be the image of a base vertex v. Then the subgroup of F(X) represented by reduced loop labels at v is the same as the subgroup represented by reduced loop labels at v′. Equivalently, after freely reducing labels of reduced loops, folding preserves the based graph language.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For one inclusion, take a reduced loop p at v in Γ. Its edgewise image p′ in Γ′ is a loop at v′ with the same label as a word before free reduction. The image path may fail to be reduced only where the two folded edges create an immediate backtrack, so reducing p′ as a path gives a reduced loop at v′ whose label represents the same element of F(X). Thus every represented element before folding is represented after folding. Conversely, suppose p′ is a reduced loop at v′ in Γ′. Let e be the new folded edge, obtained from preimages e1,e2 in Γ. Decompose p′ into subpaths p0 f0 p1 f1 ... fk pk+1, where each fi is e or e^{-1} and the pi do not use e. The subpaths pi can be regarded in Γ away from the folded edge. For each occurrence fi=e or e^{-1}, choose the corresponding preimage di among e1,e2 or e1^{-1},e2^{-1} that connects the terminal vertex of pi to the initial vertex of p_{i+1} in Γ. Replacing each fi by di yields a path p in Γ from v to v with the same label as p′. The construction preserves reducedness because p′ was reduced and the only possible new cancellations would correspond to forbidden adjacent inverse occurrences at the folded edge. Hence every represented element after folding was already represented before folding.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For an X-digraph Γ and a vertex v, define Core(Γ,v) to be the union of all reduced paths in Γ from v to v. Then Core(Γ,v) is a connected subgraph containing v; it has no degree-one vertices except possibly v; and L(Core(Γ,v),v)=L(Γ,v), where L denotes the set of labels of reduced loops at the base vertex.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The core is a union of loops all based at v, so it is connected and contains v. Since every edge in the core lies on a reduced loop based at v, a non-base vertex of degree one cannot occur: a reduced loop entering such a vertex would have to leave immediately along the inverse edge, contradicting reducedness. The equality of languages is immediate from the definition: every reduced loop at v in Γ is included in the core, and every reduced loop in the core is a reduced loop in Γ.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let H be a finitely generated subgroup of the free group F(X), where X is finite. Then there exists a finite based X-digraph (Γ,v) such that: Γ is folded and connected; every vertex except possibly v has degree greater than one; every vertex has degree at most 2|X|; Γ is a core graph with respect to v; and L(Γ,v)=H, where L(Γ,v) is the subgroup of F(X) represented by reduced loop labels at v. If H is trivial, Γ may be the single vertex graph.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose freely reduced generators h1,...,hm of H. Start with a wedge of m oriented circles at a common vertex v1, subdividing the i-th circle into |hi| edges and labeling it so that reading around it gives hi. The reduced loop labels in this initial graph represent exactly the subgroup generated by the hi. If the graph is not folded, perform a Stallings folding. By Lemma 2.5, connectedness is preserved and the number of edges decreases; by Lemma 3.4, the represented subgroup is preserved. Since the initial graph is finite, the folding process terminates in a finite folded graph Γ. Foldedness gives the degree bound 2|X|. To see Γ is a core graph, take any final edge e and trace it back to an original edge e1 in the wedge of circles. The original edge e1 lies on a generator loop with freely reduced label. Its image in the final graph is a loop through e with the same freely reduced label. If the final graph had a non-base degree-one vertex u incident to e, any loop through e would have to traverse e and immediately traverse e^{-1}, forcing a non-free-reduced label, a contradiction. Thus no non-base degree-one vertices occur, and the graph is core with respect to the base.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.9
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a connected folded X-digraph that is a core graph with respect to a base vertex v, and let H=L(Γ,v) ≤ F(X). Then: (1) for every initial segment w of a freely reduced word h in H, there is a unique reduced path in Γ with origin v and label w; (2) for every reduced path p in Γ with origin v and label w, the word w occurs as an initial segment, hence as a subword, of some freely reduced word h in H.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For (1), since h is accepted by Γ, there is a reduced loop at v with label h; its initial segment of length |w| gives the required path. Uniqueness follows from foldedness: at each vertex there is at most one outgoing edge of any given label. For (2), let p end at u. If u=v, then w itself represents an element of H. If u≠v, the core property implies u has degree at least two. Choose an edge e starting at u that is not the inverse of the last edge of p. Since Γ is connected, choose a reduced path p′ from t(e) to v. Then p e p′ is a reduced loop at v whose label is freely reduced because Γ is folded; this label lies in H and has w as an initial segment.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ and Δ be connected X-digraphs, and assume Δ is folded. Fix vertices v in Γ and u in Δ. Then there is at most one morphism of X-digraphs f:Γ→Δ such that f(v)=u, where a morphism sends vertices to vertices, directed edges to directed edges, and preserves origins, termini, directions, and labels.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For any vertex v′ of Γ, choose a reduced path p from v to v′ and let w be its label. If f and j are two morphisms Γ→Δ sending v to u, then f(p) and j(p) are paths in Δ starting at u with the same label w. Because Δ is folded, there is at most one such path, so f(v′)=j(v′). Thus the two morphisms agree on vertices. Since labels and endpoints determine the image of each edge in a folded target, they agree on edges as well.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K≤H≤F(X). Suppose (Γ1,v1) and (Γ2,v2) are connected folded based X-digraphs, each a core graph with respect to its base vertex, such that L(Γ1,v1)=K and L(Γ2,v2)=H. Then there exists a unique morphism of X-digraphs π:Γ1→Γ2 with π(v1)=v2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Uniqueness is Lemma 4.2. For existence, define π on vertices by path labels. For a vertex q of Γ1, choose a reduced path pq from v1 to q and let w=μ(pq). By Lemma 3.9, w is an initial segment of a freely reduced word in K, hence of a word in H. Since Γ2 is folded and core with language H, Lemma 3.9 gives a unique path in Γ2 from v2 with label w; define π(q) to be its endpoint. This does not depend on the chosen path pq: if p′q has label w′, then pq(p′q)^{-1} is a loop at v1 whose label represents an element of K≤H, and following the corresponding loop in Γ2 shows the endpoints obtained from w and w′ coincide; foldedness supplies uniqueness. For an edge e of Γ1 with label x, the core property puts e on a reduced loop at v1 whose label lies in K≤H. Reading the same loop in Γ2 forces an edge with label x from π(o(e)) to π(t(e)); set π(e) equal to that edge. This yields a label-preserving graph morphism.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f:Γ→Δ be an epimorphism of X-digraphs. Then f factors as f=f2∘f1, where f1:Γ→Γ′ is injective on the edge set of Γ and f2:Γ′→Δ is a bijection on vertex sets. Informally, f1 only identifies vertices of Γ that f identifies, while f2 then identifies edges with the same image in Δ. If Γ is finite, the second step may be viewed as a finite sequence of generalized foldings.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Construct Γ′ by collapsing each fiber of the vertex map VΓ→VΔ to a single vertex, without yet identifying edges. The induced map f1 is injective on the original edge set, because edges are still distinct. After this vertex identification, any two edges of Γ′ that have the same image edge in Δ have the same label and the same origin and terminus as far as Δ is concerned. The map f2 identifies exactly these edge fibers. Since the vertices have already been collapsed to the correct fibers, f2 is a bijection on vertex sets. The composite is the original epimorphism.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorems 5.1 and 5.2; Definition 5.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let H be any subgroup of the free group F(X), with X finite. There exists a based X-digraph (Γ,1_H) such that Γ is connected, folded, a core graph with respect to 1_H, and L(Γ,1_H)=H. Moreover, if (Γ1,v1) and (Γ2,v2) are two connected folded based X-digraphs, each core with respect to its base, and both have language H, then there is a unique base-preserving isomorphism Γ1→Γ2. The unique based graph is denoted Γ(H) and called the subgroup graph of H with respect to X.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For existence, build the coset X-digraph Δ of H in F(X). Its vertices are left cosets Hf, and for x in X there is an x-labeled directed edge from Hf to Hg exactly when Hfx=Hg. The base vertex is H. The graph is connected because a freely reduced word f=x1...xn gives a path H,Hx1,Hx1x2,...,Hf. It is folded because from a coset Hf and a letter x there is only one possible coset Hfx, so at most one outgoing x-edge. A freely reduced word labels a loop at H exactly when Hf=H, i.e. f∈H; hence L(Δ,H)=H. Taking Γ=Core(Δ,H) preserves the language and gives the required core graph. For uniqueness, apply Proposition 4.3 to get a base-preserving morphism Γ1→Γ2 and another Γ2→Γ1. Their composites are base-preserving morphisms from a connected graph to itself; by Lemma 4.2 each composite must be the identity. Thus the morphisms are inverse isomorphisms.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 6.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a folded connected X-digraph with base vertex v, and let T be a spanning tree of Γ. For each positive edge e of Γ not in T, define p_e to be the reduced path [v,o(e)]_T e [t(e),v]_T, where [a,b]_T is the unique reduced path in the tree T from a to b. Let [e]=μ(p_e), and set Y_T={[e] : e is a positive edge of Γ outside T}. Then Y_T is a free basis of the subgroup H=L(Γ,v)≤F(X).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Extend the notation to all oriented edges of ^hatΓ by p_e=[v,o(e)]_T e [t(e),v]_T and [e]=μ(p_e); then [e^{-1}]=[e]^{-1}. If e lies in the tree T, the path p_e reduces to the trivial path, so [e]=1. For generation, take any reduced loop p=e1...ek at v. Insert the tree paths from v to each intermediate vertex and back; after path reductions, p is obtained from the concatenation p_{e1}...p_{ek}. Thus its label is the product [e1]...[ek], and tree edges contribute only the identity, so H is generated by Y_T. For freeness, consider a nonempty freely reduced word [e1]...[ek] in symbols from Y_T^{±1}, with ei not equal to e_{i+1}^{-1}. The corresponding path reduces to [v,o(e1)]_T e1 [t(e1),o(e2)]_T e2 ... ek [t(ek),v]_T. This path is reduced: the non-tree edges ei cannot cancel with tree paths, and adjacent non-tree edges would cancel only if ei=e_{i+1}^{-1}, which is excluded. Since Γ is folded, its label is a nontrivial freely reduced word in F(X). Hence no nontrivial reduced word in Y_T represents the identity.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 6.2 and Corollary 6.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a connected folded X-digraph, let Γ′ be a connected subgraph containing a vertex v, and set G=L(Γ,v), H=L(Γ′,v). Then H is a free factor of G. If Γ′ does not contain Core(Γ,v), then H≠G. In particular, if both Γ and Γ′ are core graphs with respect to v and Γ′ is a proper subgraph of Γ, then L(Γ′,v) is a proper free factor of L(Γ,v).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose a spanning tree T′ of Γ′ and extend it to a spanning tree T of Γ. Lemma 6.1 gives a free basis Y_T for G from positive edges outside T. This basis splits as the disjoint union of the basis Y_{T′} coming from positive edges of Γ′ outside T′ and a set Z coming from positive edges outside Γ′. Therefore G=F(Y_{T′}) * F(Z), while H=F(Y_{T′}), so H is a free factor. If Γ′ does not contain Core(Γ,v), then there is a positive edge of Γ outside Γ′ that lies on a reduced loop based at v; such an edge cannot be contained entirely in the tree part attached outside Γ′. Thus Z is nonempty, and the free product decomposition is proper.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 6.6, Proposition 6.7, and Corollary 6.8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a connected graph with base vertex v. A spanning tree T is geodesic relative to v if, for every vertex u, the tree path [v,u]_T has the smallest possible length among all paths in Γ from v to u. Such a geodesic spanning tree exists. If Γ is a folded X-digraph that is a core graph with respect to v, H=L(Γ,v), and T is a geodesic spanning tree, then the free basis Y_T from Lemma 6.1 is Nielsen-reduced with respect to X: for u,w in Y_T ∪ Y_T^{-1} with u≠w^{-1}, the freely reduced product u·w has length at least both |u|_X and |w|_X; and for u,w,z in Y_T ∪ Y_T^{-1} with u≠w^{-1} and z≠w^{-1}, the product u·w·z has length greater than |u|_X+|z|_X−|w|_X. Consequently every subgroup of F(X) is free, and it has a Nielsen-reduced free basis.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For existence of T, build it by breadth-first layers. Let T0 be the base vertex v. Having built a geodesic tree on the ball of radius n−1, attach to every vertex at distance n one edge from a predecessor at distance n−1. The union over all n is a geodesic spanning tree. For Nielsen reducedness, write for an oriented non-tree edge e the loop p_e=A_e e B_e, where A_e=[v,o(e)]_T and B_e=[t(e),v]_T. Since T is geodesic and o(e),t(e) are adjacent in Γ, the lengths of A_e and B_e differ by at most one, and each of A_e, B_e, A_e e, and e B_e is controlled by approximately one half of |p_e|. For a product [e][f] with e≠f^{-1}, the path p_e p_f reduces to A_e e [t(e),o(f)]_T f B_f; the non-tree edges survive. Geodesicity and triangle inequalities compare the middle tree segment with the omitted tree segments and show the remaining path has length at least |p_e| and at least |p_f|, giving the first Nielsen condition. For a triple [e][f][g] with e≠f^{-1} and g≠f^{-1}, the reduced path is A_e e [t(e),o(f)]_T f [t(f),o(g)]_T g B_g. When p_e p_f p_g is reduced to this path, at most |A_f| letters can cancel from the end of p_e and at most |B_f| letters from the beginning of p_g, while the central edge f survives. Since |p_f|=|A_f|+1+|B_f|, the final length is at least 1+|p_e|+|p_g|−|A_f|−|B_f|, which is strictly greater than |p_e|+|p_g|−|p_f|. Lemma 6.1 supplies freeness, and applying the construction to the subgroup graph Γ(H) gives a Nielsen-reduced basis for any subgroup H≤F(X).

proof_techniques.name: Language-preserving Stallings fold
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly uses the local operation of folding two equally labeled edges with the same initial vertex. This operation is purely combinatorial, decreases the number of edges in a finite graph, and preserves the subgroup represented by based reduced loops. The preservation proof has two directions: images of loops remain loops after possible path reduction, and reduced loops in the folded graph can be lifted by choosing appropriate preimages of each folded edge.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A reducible word with a matching can potentially be encoded as a cancellation diagram or graph; a local folding move could serve as a canonical complexity-reducing move on such diagrams. If the cells of F_w can be interpreted as choices among local resolutions of folds or cancellations, this technique suggests a route to a deformation retraction or discrete Morse matching.

proof_techniques.name: Fold until deterministic
proof_techniques.description: Starting from a wedge of loops labeled by subgroup generators, the paper folds all non-deterministic pairs of edges until the graph is folded. Finiteness guarantees termination because each fold reduces the positive edge count. The terminal folded graph has deterministic path-reading: from any vertex and label there is at most one path with that label.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Different matchings of the same reducible word may be viewed as different cancellation histories. A proof of contractibility might try to show that all histories flow to a common deterministic normal form, analogously to folding all graphs representing the same subgroup to the same folded core.

proof_techniques.name: Core extraction
proof_techniques.description: The core Core(Γ,v) is the union of all reduced loops based at v. Removing everything outside the core does not change the based language, and the resulting graph has no non-base degree-one vertices. This separates essential cancellation data from hanging tree-like data that carries no closed reduced loop labels.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a matching diagram, arcs or regions that do not contribute to a closed cancellation pattern might be removable without changing the represented free-group triviality. The no-degree-one feature is a useful analogue for ruling out degenerate local configurations.

proof_techniques.name: Deterministic path-reading and canonical morphisms
proof_techniques.description: Folded graphs behave like deterministic automata: a starting vertex and a word determine at most one path. This gives uniqueness of graph morphisms into folded graphs, and subgroup inclusion K≤H yields a canonical base-preserving morphism Γ(K)→Γ(H) by sending a vertex to the endpoint of the same path label in Γ(H).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If one can associate to a partial matching a folded or deterministic state, prefixes of the fixed word w may determine canonical endpoints or canonical next moves. Such uniqueness can reduce choices and help prove that a space of choices is contractible.

proof_techniques.name: Morphism factorization into vertex identifications and folds
proof_techniques.description: Any epimorphism of X-digraphs factors first by identifying vertices and then by identifying edges over the same image; in finite cases the second stage is a sequence of generalized foldings. Thus a global quotient map can be decomposed into elementary local moves.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The cell attachments in F_w are defined by resolving one crossing at a time. If matchings can be encoded as quotients of a graph associated to w, this factorization suggests comparing global changes of matchings to sequences of elementary local resolutions or folds.

proof_techniques.name: Spanning-tree normal form for loop labels
proof_techniques.description: Given a folded connected based graph and a spanning tree, each non-tree positive edge determines a loop based at the base vertex. The labels of these loops form a free basis for the subgroup represented by the graph. The proof expresses any reduced loop as a product of these edge loops and proves no nontrivial relation by observing that a reduced word in non-tree edges yields a genuinely reduced path with nontrivial label.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A matching diagram may have an underlying graph whose independent cycles correspond to independent cancellation choices. A spanning-tree decomposition could isolate independent parameters and help analyze cubical coordinates or prove that certain subcomplexes are products or collapsible.

proof_techniques.name: Geodesic-tree cancellation estimates
proof_techniques.description: Using a geodesic spanning tree, the paper obtains Nielsen-reduced bases and explicit bounds on how much cancellation can occur in products of basis elements. The central estimates compare tree-path lengths on the two sides of a non-tree edge and show that in products and triples, specified non-tree edges survive while cancellation is bounded by adjacent geodesic segments.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The quasi-reduced hypothesis in the original problem forbids the local pattern ℓℓ^{-1}ℓ, which is a constraint on overlapping cancellations. The Nielsen estimates provide a template for proving that a chosen local cancellation survives or that excessive overlap would force a forbidden short pattern.

other_useful_info: Convention 2.2 is important: every positive X-labeled edge is accompanied by a formal inverse edge labeled by X^{-1}, so paths naturally carry words in X ∪ X^{-1}. Remark 2.8 identifies folded based X-digraphs with deterministic automata over X ∪ X^{-1}; for finite folded graphs, the accepted freely reduced language is regular because freely reduced words form a regular language and regular languages are closed under intersection. The paper points to Stallings, Topology of finite graphs [43], as the original source for the folding method, and Nielsen's work [36,38] for subgroup bases in free groups.
why_relevant: Gives a detailed combinatorial treatment of folding methods in free groups. Although graph foldings differ from boundary matchings, the paper provides standard free-group folding and cancellation techniques that may inform a proof.
